text.title: Scope Change vs Scope Creep Detection Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding the scope-change-vs-scope-creep-detection methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Classifier consumes exactly one signed baseline document (spec + AC + out-of-scope list). Multiple candidates or 'latest chat' are forbidden as ground truth.
rule.rationale: A classifier consulting shifting context produces shifting decisions; the signed baseline is the project's constitution.

rule.statement: Classifier returns exactly one of: in_scope / legitimate_change / creep / ambiguous. Probabilistic continuous output is forbidden as routing.
rule.rationale: Continuous scores invite negotiation; discrete states are auditable.

rule.statement: Every classifier decision MUST cite specific baseline lines (spec section, AC ID, out-of-scope clause). Decisions without citations are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Citations make the decision defensible to the requester; black-box verdicts collapse trust on the first false-positive.

rule.statement: PM (or named delegate) can override any classifier decision with a recorded reason. Override is logged; classifier must be retrained or rule-adjusted if override rate exceeds 20%.
rule.rationale: Un-overridable automation is tyranny; constantly overridden automation is decoration.

rule.statement: Every triage decision is written to an immutable audit log (ticket id, baseline version, citations, verdict, override, timestamp). Queryable at engagement close.
rule.rationale: Without audit trail, retro-claims of 'you accepted creep' or 'you blocked work' cannot be settled.

list.item: Spec / baseline is implicit or non-existent — define it first
Vendor / data-residency restrictions prevent ticket text being sent to an LLM — use rules-only triage
Team explicitly accepts 'everything is in-scope' (T&M billing) — nothing to gate
